channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: WHO’s First Global Report on Nicotine Pouches: Harm Reduction Questions Remain Amid Global Regulatory Warning
item.description: The WHO’s first global report on nicotine pouches has intensified debate around the role of non-combustible nicotine products in tobacco harm reduction policy. While the report raises concerns related to youth uptake, product regulation, and long-term health uncertainties, critics argue that the discussion gives insufficient attention to the relative risk differences between nicotine pouches and combustible cigarettes. Researchers and harm reduction advocates continue to emphasize the importance of distinguishing between nicotine use and the harms caused primarily by tobacco combustion. The debate reflects broader disagreements over how public health institutions should balance precautionary regulation with evidence-based approaches to reducing smoking-related disease and mortality. (Published on May 18, 2026by2firsts.com)

item.title: WHO chooses ideology over Sweden’s evidence on nicotine
item.description: Sweden’s experience with low smoking prevalence continues to play a central role in debates surrounding tobacco harm reduction and nicotine regulation. Critics argue that current international policy discussions often overlook epidemiological evidence showing significant reductions in smoking-related disease in populations with widespread use of non-combustible nicotine products. The debate highlights growing tensions between precautionary approaches to nicotine regulation and harm reduction models focused on reducing exposure to combustion-related toxicants. Questions remain over whether public health policy should prioritize nicotine abstinence alone or broader strategies aimed at accelerating the decline of cigarette smoking and smoking-related mortality. (Published on May 18, 2026bysnusforumet.se)

item.title: Spain Slammed for Protecting Smoking
item.description: Spain’s proposed restrictions on vaping and other reduced-risk nicotine products have intensified criticism from harm reduction advocates and public health commentators. Critics argue that treating non-combustible nicotine products in the same way as cigarettes risks obscuring important differences in toxicological exposure and relative risk. Concerns have also been raised that increasingly restrictive regulation may discourage smokers from switching away from combustible tobacco, potentially slowing declines in smoking prevalence. The debate reflects wider tensions across Europe between precautionary nicotine regulation, smoking cessation goals, and evidence-based approaches to tobacco harm reduction. (Published on May 18, 2026byplanetofthevapes.co.uk)

item.title: How to Get Less Wrong, Nicotine Edition
item.description: Understanding nicotine-related risk requires distinguishing between the harms caused by nicotine itself and those caused primarily by tobacco combustion. Public debate and media coverage often present nicotine as uniformly harmful, despite substantial differences in risk profiles between combustible and non-combustible products. Researchers and harm reduction advocates increasingly argue that inaccurate risk perception may contribute to persistent smoking by discouraging smokers from switching to lower-risk alternatives. Improving public understanding of relative risk remains a central challenge in evidence-based tobacco harm reduction and public health communication. (Published on May 18, 2026bysubstack.com)

item.title: World No Tobacco Day and the Public Health Industry That Lost the Plot
item.description: Understanding nicotine-related risk requires distinguishing between the harms caused by nicotine itself and those caused primarily by tobacco combustion. Public debate and media coverage often present nicotine as uniformly harmful, despite substantial differences in risk profiles between combustible and non-combustible products. Researchers and harm reduction advocates increasingly argue that inaccurate risk perception may contribute to persistent smoking by discouraging smokers from switching to lower-risk alternatives. Improving public understanding of relative risk remains a central challenge in evidence-based tobacco harm reduction and public health communication. (Published on May 18, 2026bysubstack.com)
